Photos, videos, witness statements, or incident evidence.
During an emergency, information shall be shared quickly with those who need it to protect life,
safety, environment, security, product integrity, or legal compliance. After the emergency is controlled,
information shall be managed according to HR, HSE, QA, Security, Legal/Compliance, and
document-control requirements.
4. Emergency Response Principles
BSF shall manage emergencies according to the following principles:
Principle Requirement
Life safety ﬁrst Protecting people is the ﬁrst priority in every emergency.
Immediate reporting Emergencies, alarms, injuries, spills, ﬁre, security events, and unsafe
conditions shall be reported immediately.
Stop and assess Personnel shall not attempt respon

larms, injuries, spills, ﬁre, security events, and unsafe
conditions shall be reported immediately.
Stop and assess Personnel shall not attempt response actions beyond their training,
authority, or personal safety.
Clear command Emergency response shall follow deﬁned roles and escalation routes.
Evacuation discipline When evacuation is ordered, personnel shall leave immediately through
safe routes and report to assembly points.
No re-entry No person shall re-enter an evacuated area until authorized by the
Emergency Coordinator or competent authority.
Product protection
after people
Food safety, product integrity, traceability, and cargo security shall be
assessed after people are safe.
Evidence and learning Emergencies and drills shall be recorded, investigated, and used to
improve the system.

8. Emergency Roles and Responsibilities
8.1 Emergency Coordinator
The Emergency Coordinator is responsible for:
Taking command of the initial site emergency response.
Conﬁrming the type and level of emergency.
Activating alarms, evacuation, shelter-in-place, or restricted-area control as appropriate.
Coordinating with HSE, Security, Operations, QA, HR, and external emergency services.
Ensuring headcount is completed.
Authorizing re-entry only when safe and appropriate.
Ensuring incident records and post-event actions are initiated.

# Page 8
8.2 Site Manager
The Site Manager is responsible for:
Providing leadership and resources during emergencies.
Escalating major events to senior management.
Coordinating business continuity and recovery decisions.
Supporting communication with authorities, customers, corporate stakeholders, insurers, or
certiﬁcation bodies when required.
Ensuring corrective actions are implemented after the emergency.
8.3 HSE Coordinator
The HSE Coordinator is responsible for:
Maintaining emergency response arrangements.
Training personnel on emergency procedures.
Coordinating drills.
Maintaining emergency equipment inspection records.
Supporting incident investigation.
Advising on safety controls, medical response, spill response, evacuation, and regulatory
reporting.
8.4 Security Team or Security Coordinator
Security is responsible for:
Controlling access during emergencies.
Directing emergency

gulatory
reporting.
8.4 Security Team or Security Coordinator
Security is responsible for:
Controlling access during emergencies.
Directing emergency services to the correct entrance.
Preventing unauthorized entry.
Supporting evacuation ﬂow.
Protecting restricted areas, cargo zones, sensitive records, and BASC-related controls.
Maintaining visitor and contractor logs for headcount.
Reporting suspicious activity or security threats.
8.5 First Aiders
First Aiders are responsible for:
Providing ﬁrst aid within their training limits.
Activating medical emergency escalation when needed.

# Page 9
Protecting themselves from exposure.
Recording ﬁrst-aid attention according to site requirements.
Coordinating with HR and HSE after the response.
8.6 Fire Wardens or Area Wardens

Supporting orderly evacuation.
Checking assigned areas only if safe to do so.
Directing people to emergency exits.
Reporting blocked exits, missing persons, or hazards to the Emergency Coordinator.
Not placing themselves at risk during area checks.

Assessing food safety and product impact after emergencies.
Placing aﬀected product, materials, packaging, or equipment on hold when necessary.
Evaluating contamination, temperature deviation, water exposure, smoke exposure, chemical
exposure, pest exposure, or traceability impact.
Coordinating product release, disposal, cleaning, sanitation, re

Coordinating product release, disposal, cleaning, sanitation, recall, or withdrawal decisions

Supporting recovery only after authorization.

Reporting emergencies immediately.

9. Emergency Communication and Escalation
Emergency communication shall be fast, clear, and controlled. The ﬁrst person discovering an
emergency shall immediately:
1. Protect themselves.
2. Warn nearby persons.
3. Activate the alarm or notify the designated emergency contact.
4. Report the location, type of emergency, persons aﬀected, immediate hazards, and whether
emergency services are needed.
The minimum information to communicate is:
What happened.
Exact location.
Time observed.
People injured, trapped, missing, or exposed.
Fire, smoke, spill, ch

imum information to communicate is:
What happened.
Exact location.
Time observed.
People injured, trapped, missing, or exposed.
Fire, smoke, spill, chemical, equipment, security, or utility hazard involved.
Actions already taken.
Whether evacuation or area isolation has started.
Name and contact of person reporting.
External communication to media, customers, authorities, or community stakeholders shall be
handled only by authorized management, Legal/Compliance, Communications, or designated
representatives.
10. Alarm Activation
Emergency alarms shall be activated when there is or may be immediate risk to people, such as ﬁre,
smoke, gas release, chemical exposure, explosion risk, structural risk, serious injury, or a situation
requiring evacuation.

# Page 11
If an alarm sounds:
Stop work immediately where safe.
Secure equipment only if it can be done quickly and safely.
Leave by the nearest safe exit.
Follow designated evacuation routes.
Assist visitors or persons requiring help if safe.
Go directly to the assembly point.
Report to the supervisor or warden for headcount.
Do not re-enter until authorized.
False alarms shall still be treated as real until the Emergency Coordinator conﬁrms otherwise.

# Page 12
Evacuation routes, emergency exits, assembly points, alarms, and ﬁre equipment shall be clearly
marked and communicated through induction, signage, and drills.
12. Assembly Point and Headcount
Assembly points shall be located away from immediate hazards, vehicle routes, smoke, chemical
exposure, unstable structures, and emergency-service access routes.
At the assembly point:
Employees shall remain with their department or group.
Supervisors shall conﬁrm presence of their team.
Security or Reception shall provide visitor and contractor logs when available.
HR may support headcount reconciliation.
Missing persons shall be reported immediately to the Emergency Coordinator.
No person shall leave the assembly point without authorization.
Headcount must include employees, contractors, visitors, drivers, auditors, inspectors, and any other
person signed into the site.
13. Fire Emergen

n.
Headcount must include employees, contractors, visitors, drivers, auditors, inspectors, and any other
person signed into the site.
13. Fire Emergency
If ﬁre or smoke is discovered:
Raise the alarm immediately.
Warn nearby persons.
Evacuate the area.
Close doors if safe to do so.
Do not attempt to ﬁght the ﬁre unless trained, authorized, the ﬁre is small, the correct
extinguisher is available, there is a safe exit behind you, and the action does not place you at
risk.
Do not use water on electrical or incompatible chemical ﬁres.
Do not re-enter the area.
Fire wardens shall support evacuation but shall not conduct risky searches or ﬁreﬁghting beyond their
training.
After a ﬁre event, QA shall assess whether product, packaging, raw materials, labels, utensils, food-
contact surfaces, or storage areas were aﬀected by smoke, heat, water, extinguisher residue, debris,

# Page 13
structural damage, or loss of temperature control.
14. Medical Emergency and First Aid
In a medical emergency:
Call for ﬁrst aid and notify the supervisor.
Do not move the injured person unless there is immediate danger.
Keep the area safe.
Provide ﬁrst aid only within training limits.
Call external emergency medical services when required.
Send someone to guide emergency responders to the location.
Protect the injured person’s privacy and dignity.
Report the incident to HSE and HR.
Medical emergencies include serious bleeding, loss of consciousness, electric shock, suspected
fracture, chemical exposure, burns, chest pain, breathing diﬃculty, severe allergic reaction, seizure,
heat stress, cold stress, crushing injury, or any condition requiring urgent medical attention.
15. Chemical Spill or Hazardous Material Release
If a chemical spill or hazardous material release occurs:
S

ition requiring urgent medical attention.
15. Chemical Spill or Hazardous Material Release
If a chemical spill or hazardous material release occurs:
Stop work in the aﬀected area.
Avoid contact with the substance.
Warn nearby persons.
Notify the supervisor, HSE, and Security where applicable.
Identify the substance only if it can be done safely.
Refer to the Safety Data Sheet.
Isolate the area.
Use spill kits only if trained and if the spill is within site response capability.
Prevent spread to drains, product, packaging, food-contact areas, or public areas where safe.
Evacuate if fumes, ﬁre risk, unknown chemical, or exposure risk exists.
Chemical spills that may aﬀect product, packaging, water, drains, air, employees, neighboring areas,
or the environment shall be escalated immediately.

# Page 14
16. Refrigeration, Cold-Chain, and Temperature-
Control Emergency
Cold-chain emergencies may include refrigeration failure, freezer door failure, ammonia or refrigerant
leak suspicion, extended power outage, temperature excursion, trapped person in cold room, ice
buildup, or uncontrolled condensation.
Immediate actions may include:
Notify the supervisor, Maintenance, QA, HSE, and Operations.
Restrict access to aﬀected cold areas where safety is uncertain.
Evacuate if there is suspected refrigerant leak, oxygen displacement, strong odor, breathing
diﬃculty, or alarm activation.
Verify no person is trapped inside cold rooms.
Protect workers from cold stress.
Segregate or place aﬀected product on hold as instructed by QA.
Record temperature data and time of failure.
Do not release aﬀected product until QA completes assessment.
Where certiﬁed product is aﬀected, QA shall assess MSC

erature data and time of failure.
Do not release aﬀected product until QA completes assessment.
Where certiﬁed product is aﬀected, QA shall assess MSC Chain of Custody and traceability
implications.
17. Severe Weather and Natural Hazards
Sites shall prepare for natural hazards relevant to their location, such as heavy rain, ﬂooding,
earthquake, extreme heat, lightning, strong wind, landslide, tsunami risk, or other regional hazards.
General response principles include:
Follow oﬃcial alerts and site instructions.
Secure loose materials where safe.
Avoid ﬂooded areas, unstable structures, damaged wiring, or fallen objects.
Move to designated safe areas if evacuation is not appropriate.
Stop outdoor work during lightning or unsafe weather.
Protect critical records, chemicals, product, and equipment only after people are safe.
Report structural damage, water ingress, contamination risk, or u

tect critical records, chemicals, product, and equipment only after people are safe.
Report structural damage, water ingress, contamination risk, or utility failure.
After severe weather, re-entry shall occur only after safety assessment.

# Page 15
18. Earthquake Response
During an earthquake:
Drop, cover, and hold where appropriate.
Move away from windows, shelves, racks, glass, heavy objects, or unstable materials.
Do not run during shaking.
After shaking stops, evacuate if instructed or if conditions are unsafe.
Avoid elevators.
Watch for aftershocks.
Report injuries, trapped persons, ﬁres, gas odors, chemical spills, structural damage, or blocked
exits.
QA shall assess product and facility impact after any event aﬀecting structures, utilities, storage
racks, water systems, packaging, or controlled areas.
19. Utility Failure
Utility failures may include electricity, water, refrigeration, compressed air, internet, drainage,
ventilation, gas, fuel, or communication systems.
Supervisors shall assess whether work can continue safely. Work shall stop if the utility failure aﬀects:
Lighting required for safe work.
Refrigerat

Supervisors shall assess whether work can continue safely. Work shall stop if the utility failure aﬀects:
Lighting required for safe work.
Refrigeration or cold-chain control.
Water for hygiene, cleaning, sanitation, or welfare.
Ventilation for chemical or conﬁned areas.
Equipment safety controls.
Fire detection or alarm systems.
Emergency communication.
Food safety or product integrity.
Security or access control.
QA, HSE, Maintenance, Operations, Security, and IT shall coordinate response according to the
aﬀected utility.

# Page 16
20. Security-Related Emergency
Security-related emergencies may include unauthorized access, suspicious package, threat, violence,
civil disturbance, cargo tampering, seal breach, theft, sabotage, cyber-supported operational
disruption, or suspicious activity near restricted areas.
Personnel shall:
Avoid confrontation unless trained and authorized.
Move away from immediate danger.
Notify Security or supervisor immediately.
Preserve evidence where safe.
Follow evacuation, shelter-in-place, or lockdown instructions.
Protect cargo, documents, seals, access cards, and sensitive information only if safe.
Security shall coordinate with site management, HSE, HR, QA, Legal/Compliance, and external
authorities where required. BASC-related incidents shall be documented and reviewed for supply-
chain security impact.
21. Food Safety or Product Protection Emergency
Some emergencies may cre

incidents shall be documented and reviewed for supply-
chain security impact.
21. Food Safety or Product Protection Emergency
Some emergencies may create food safety or product integrity risks. Examples include:
Fire, smoke, or extinguisher residue.
Chemical spill near product or packaging.
Flooding or water intrusion.
Pest intrusion after structural damage.
Loss of refrigeration.
Broken glass or foreign material release.
Sewage or drainage backup.
Employee illness incident in hygiene-controlled area.
Security breach aﬀecting certiﬁed product or cargo.
Utility failure aﬀecting sanitation or temperature control.
QA shall place aﬀected product, packaging, equipment, or area on hold until assessment is
complete. No aﬀected material shall be released without QA authorization.

# Page 17
22. Environmental Release
Environmental releases may include chemical discharge, fuel spill, wastewater release, refrigerant
leak, uncontrolled waste, contaminated water, or release to drains, soil, air, or neighboring areas.
Immediate response shall include:
Protect people ﬁrst.
Stop the release if trained and safe.
Prevent spread where safe.
Notify HSE immediately.
Isolate drains or aﬀected areas where possible.
Use spill materials only if trained.
Escalate to external authorities where required by law.
Document the event and corrective actions.
Environmental response shall be coordinated with HSE, Maintenance, Operations, Legal/Compliance,
and site management.
23. Contractor and Visitor Emergency Control
Contractors and visitors shall receive emergency instructions before entering controlled work areas.
The level of instruction shall match the visit or work risk.
At minimum,

s shall receive emergency instructions before entering controlled work areas.
The level of instruction shall match the visit or work risk.
At minimum, contractors and visitors shall know:
Alarm meaning.
Evacuation route.
Assembly point.
Escort or contact person.
Restricted areas.
PPE requirements.
Emergency reporting method.
Prohibition on re-entry after evacuation.
Contractor supervisors shall account for their personnel during emergencies and report headcount to
BSF’s designated representative.

# Page 18
24. Emergency Equipment
Emergency equipment shall be appropriate to site risk and may include:
Fire extinguishers.
Fire alarms and detectors.
Emergency lighting.
First-aid kits.
Eyewash stations and safety showers.
Spill kits.
Emergency communication devices.
Evacuation maps.
Emergency exits and signage.
Cold-room emergency release devices.
Rescue equipment where required.
PPE for response teams.
Emergency equipment shall be inspected according to site schedule, legal requirement, and
manufacturer recommendations. Defective, missing, expired, blocked, or discharged equipment shall
be reported and corrected promptly.
25. Emergency Drills
Emergency drills shall be conducted to test readiness, improve response, and verify that personnel
understand emergency procedures.
Drills may include:
Evacuation drill.
Fire drill.
Chemical spill drill.
Medical emergency drill.
Cold-room trappe

nel
understand emergency procedures.
Drills may include:
Evacuation drill.
Fire drill.
Chemical spill drill.
Medical emergency drill.
Cold-room trapped-person scenario.
Refrigeration failure scenario.
Security incident scenario.
Severe weather or earthquake drill.
Product-hold emergency scenario.

# Page 19
Drills shall be planned, documented, evaluated, and reviewed. Improvement actions shall be
assigned owners and deadlines.
26. Training and Awareness
Emergency response training shall be provided according to role and risk. At minimum, employees
shall receive emergency awareness during onboarding and periodic refreshers.
Training topics may include:
Alarm response.
Evacuation routes.
Assembly points.
Emergency contacts.
Fire prevention.
First-aid reporting.
Spill reporting.
Cold-room emergency actions.
Security incident reporting.
Food safety emergency escalation.
Stop-work authority.
Visitor and contractor emergency control.
Specialized training shall be provided for ﬁrst aiders, ﬁre wardens, spill responders, emergency
coordinators, security personnel, maintenance personnel, forklift operators, chemical users, and cold-
chain personnel.
27. Post-Emergency Recovery
After the em

rs, security personnel, maintenance personnel, forklift operators, chemical users, and cold-
chain personnel.
27. Post-Emergency Recovery
After the emergency is controlled, recovery shall be coordinated before normal operations resume.
Recovery actions may include:
Conﬁrming all persons are safe.
Securing the aﬀected area.
Preserving the scene for investigation where required.
Conducting safety assessment before re-entry.
Inspecting equipment, structures, utilities, drains, alarms, and emergency systems.
Assessing product, packaging, raw material, certiﬁed product, and records impact.

# Page 20
Cleaning and sanitizing aﬀected areas.
Disposing of contaminated material.
Restoring utilities.
Communicating return-to-work instructions.
Documenting losses, damage, and corrective actions.
Operations shall not resume until the responsible manager, HSE, QA, Security, Maintenance, or
external authority conﬁrms that restart conditions are acceptable.
28. Incident Investigation and Corrective Action
Emergencies, signiﬁcant near misses, drill failures, evacuation issues, emergency equipment failures,
and serious response gaps shall be investigated.
The investigation shall identify:
What happened.
Immediate and root causes.
Persons aﬀected.
Response timeline.
Communication eﬀectiveness.
Equipment performance.
Training or awareness gaps.
Control failures.
Product, environmental, security, or business impact.
Corrective and preventive actions.
Corrective actions shall be assigned, tr

s.
Control failures.
Product, environmental, security, or business impact.
Corrective and preventive actions.
Corrective actions shall be assigned, tracked, and veriﬁed for eﬀectiveness. Lessons learned shall be
communicated where appropriate.

36. Appendix C — Evacuation Quick Guide
When the alarm sounds:
1. Stop work.
2. Make the task safe only if it can be done immediately and without risk.
3. Leave through the nearest safe exit.
4. Do not collect personal belongings.
5. Do not run.
6. Help visitors or persons requiring support if safe.
7. Go to the assembly point.
8. Report to your supervisor or warden.
9. Stay at the assembly point.
10. Do not re-enter until the all clear is given.

# Page 27
37. Appendix D — Spill Response Quick Guide
Step Action
1 Stop and protect yourself.
2 Warn others and restrict access.
3 Identify the substance only if safe.
4 Notify supervisor and HSE.
5 Check the Safety Data Sheet.
6 Use spill kit only if trained and safe.
7 Prevent spread to drains or product where safe.
8 Dispose of contaminated material according to instructions.
9 Record and report the incident.
10 Do not resume work until the area is cleared.
38. Appendix E — Product Impact Decision Trigger

Product, packaging, labels, ingredients, or raw material exposure.
Smoke, ﬁre, water, chemical, glass, pest, dust, debris, or foreign material.
Loss of refrigeration or temperature control.
Flooding, sewage, or drainage backup.
Structural damage above product or processing areas.
Cleaning chemical or fu

frigeration or temperature control.

Broken equipment that may aﬀect food-contact surfaces.
Emergency response materials used in food handling areas.
Aﬀected product shall remain on hold until QA authorizes disposition.

classificationrules.allergendeclarationrule: Fish, crustaceans, and mollusks must be declared according to customer, market, and legal labeling requirements.
classificationrules.mscclaimrule: MSC claim may only be used when the species, supplier, vessel, batch, and chain of custody evidence are approved and traceable.
classificationrules.haccprule: Each species must be linked to the applicable hazard assessment for biological, chemical, physical, allergen, and temperature control risks.

1. Purpose
The purpose of this procedure is to deﬁne how BSF receives, veriﬁes, inspects, accepts, rejects,
records, and controls seafood products at the point of receipt. The procedure establishes a
consistent method for conﬁrming that incoming seafood complies with purchase requirements,
approved supplier conditions, authorized vessel requirements, species speciﬁcations, temperature
limits, freshness criteria, traceability requirements, food safety controls, certiﬁcation claims, cargo
security controls, and applicable legal or customer requirements.
Receiving is one of the most critical control points in BSF’s operational system. Once seafood is
accepted into

cable legal or customer requirements.
Receiving is one of the most critical control points in BSF’s operational system. Once seafood is
accepted into BSF custody, the company becomes responsible for maintaining product identity,
quality, food safety, certiﬁed status, cold-chain integrity, and reliable records. For that reason, no
incoming lot shall be released to storage, processing, sale, export preparation, or customer allocation
until the minimum receiving and inspection checks deﬁned in this procedure have been completed
and recorded.
This procedure supports the prevention of:
Acceptance of product from unapproved suppliers or unauthorized vessels.
Loss of traceability or incorrect lot identiﬁcation.
Acceptance of product outside temperature requirements.
Mislabeling or species substitution.
Acceptance of damaged, contaminated, decomposed, adulterated, or unsafe product.

documentation.

Cargo security weaknesses aﬀecting BASC-related controls.
Delays in identifying nonconforming product and applying corrective action.

# Page 3
2. Scope
This procedure applies to all seafood raw material, semi-processed product, frozen product, chilled
product, packed product, samples, returns, transfers, and customer-directed product received by
BSF or by third parties operating under BSF instruction.
It applies to:
Fresh seafood delivered directly from landing points, vessels, farms, suppliers, brokers,
consolidators, or approved transporters.
Frozen seafood delivered by refrigerated truck, reefer container, cold storage transfer, or
approved logistics provider.
Chilled seafood requiring controlled temperature handling.
Products covered by MSC Chain of Custody, BRCGS Food Safety, IFS Food, HACCP, customer
standards, export requirements, or internal speciﬁcations.
Product received for processing, storage, repacking, inspection, sampling, export preparation,
customer allocation, or evaluation.
Receiving activities perfo

received for processing, storage, repacking, inspection, sampling, export preparation,
customer allocation, or evaluation.
Receiving activities performed by Operations, Quality Assurance, Food Safety, Procurement,
Warehouse, Security, Traceability, and Logistics personnel.
This procedure does not replace supplier approval, product speciﬁcation management, export
documentation, corrective action procedures, recall procedures, laboratory testing methods,
sanitation procedures, security procedures, or cold-chain monitoring instructions. When a more
speciﬁc document applies, both documents shall be followed. If there is a conﬂict, the stricter food
safety, legal, customer, certiﬁcation, or traceability requirement shall apply.
3. Conﬁdentiality
This document is classiﬁed as Internal Use Only. It may be shared with BSF employees, approved
suppliers, auditors, certiﬁcation bodies, customers,

Personnel shall not disclose receiving results, supplier ﬁndings, product defects, cargo security

notiﬁcation, or supplier escalation shall be coordinated through the responsible manager.

7. Roles and Responsibilities
7.1 Operations Manager
The Operations Manager is responsible for ensuring that receiving areas, personnel, equipment,
unloading capacity, cold-chain resources, and shift controls are available to apply this procedure. The
Operations Manager ensures that product ﬂow does not bypass inspection, product hold, or QA
release requirements.
7.2 Quality Assurance Manager
The Quality Assurance Manager is responsible for deﬁning inspection criteria, approving acceptance
decisions for quality or food safety exceptions, reviewing nonconformities, authorizing product

for deﬁning inspection criteria, approving acceptance
decisions for quality or food safety exceptions, reviewing nonconformities, authorizing product
release from hold when appropriate, and ensuring that receiving controls support HACCP, BRCGS
Food Safety, IFS Food, and customer requirements.

# Page 8
7.3 Food Safety Coordinator
The Food Safety Coordinator is responsible for reviewing receiving hazards, validating temperature
and product safety criteria, advising on high-risk species or lots, coordinating testing where required,
and escalating product safety concerns.
7.4 Receiving Supervisor
The Receiving Supervisor is responsible for coordinating unloading, checking readiness of the
receiving area, ensuring personnel follow hygiene and safety rules, conﬁrming documentation is
collected, assigning inspectors, verifying that receiving records are completed, and ensuring that
accepted, rejected, and held product are identiﬁed correctly.
7.5 Receiving Inspector or QA Inspector
The Receiving Inspector or QA Inspector is responsible for performing document checks, physical
inspection, temperature checks, organoleptic evaluation, packaging condition review, label
veriﬁcation, samp

for performing document checks, physical
inspection, temperature checks, organoleptic evaluation, packaging condition review, label
veriﬁcation, sampling, record completion, and immediate reporting of deviations.
7.6 Procurement Coordinator
The Procurement Coordinator is responsible for conﬁrming supplier approval, purchase order
alignment, expected species, expected volumes, price or commercial terms where relevant, supplier
corrective actions, and communication with suppliers when documentation or delivery conditions are
incomplete.
7.7 Traceability Oﬃcer
The Traceability Oﬃcer is responsible for verifying lot coding, source information, species identity,
certiﬁcation status, MSC Chain of Custody evidence, supplier records, vessel identity, harvest or
catch data, and system registration before product release.
7.8 Warehouse or Cold Storage Coordinator
The Warehouse or Cold Storage Coo

ty, harvest or
catch data, and system registration before product release.
7.8 Warehouse or Cold Storage Coordinator
The Warehouse or Cold Storage Coordinator is responsible for assigning storage location,
maintaining segregation, controlling product movement, preserving cold-chain conditions, and
ensuring that accepted or held product is stored according to status and temperature requirements.

# Page 9
7.9 Security Personnel
Security personnel are responsible for verifying vehicle access, driver identiﬁcation, seal condition
where applicable, cargo access points, suspicious behavior, restricted-area compliance, and
immediate escalation of BASC-related concerns.
7.10 Employees, Contractors, and Visitors
All personnel involved in receiving shall follow safety, hygiene, PPE, access, product handling, and
reporting requirements. Personnel shall not move, open, sample, relabel, rework, release, or dispose
of product unless authorized.

10. Vehicle, Container, and Cargo Security Inspection
Before unloading, Security or Receiving personnel shall inspect the transport unit where applicable.
Minimum checks include:
Vehicle identiﬁcation matches documentation.
Driver identity is conﬁrmed according to site access rules.
Seal number matches the transport document.
Seal is intact before opening.
Cargo doors, locks, hinges, panels, and access points show no signs of tampering.
Vehicle interior is clean, odor-free, dry where applicable, and free from pests, chemicals, fuel,
unrelated cargo, or contamination risk.
Refrigeration unit is operating where required.
Temperature display, if available, is checked before opening.
Product is protected from sun, rain, dust, pests, fumes, unauthorized handling,

equired.
Temperature display, if available, is checked before opening.
Product is protected from sun, rain, dust, pests, fumes, unauthorized handling, and excessive
receiving delay.
If tampering, broken seals, suspicious cargo, unauthorized stops, unknown drivers, inconsistent
routes, altered documents, or other security concerns are identiﬁed, the shipment shall not be fully
released. The Receiving Supervisor shall notify Security, Operations, QA, and the Supply Chain
Security Coordinator. Product may be placed on security hold pending investigation.

12. Temperature Control at Receiving
Temperature shall be checked immediately upon receipt or as soon as safely possible. The inspection
method shall be appropriate to product type, packaging, risk, and legal/customer requirements.
Minimum checks may include:
Vehicle or container temperature display.
Ambient temperature inside the vehicle or cargo area.
Produc

tomer requirements.
Minimum checks may include:
Vehicle or container temperature display.
Ambient temperature inside the vehicle or cargo area.
Product surface temperature.
Product core temperature where practical and required.
Temperature of representative boxes, pallets, totes, or zones.
Temperature of product at highest-risk locations, such as door area, top layer, outside pallet, or
product with visible thawing.
Review of transport temperature records where available.
Temperature results shall be recorded in the receiving record or linked cold-chain record. The
receiving team shall refer to BSF-OPS-001 — Cold Chain Control Register and product-speciﬁc
speciﬁcations for applicable limits.

13. Physical and Organoleptic Inspection
Incoming seafood shall be inspected using product-speciﬁc criteria. The Receiving Inspector or QA
Inspector shall evaluate representative samples from the lot according to risk, quantity, supplier
history, and product type.
Inspection shall consider:
Product appearance.
Odor.
Texture.
Color.
Eyes, gills, skin, scales, shell, ﬂesh, or exoskeleton condition where applicable.
Presence of slime, discoloration, dehydration, freezer burn, bruising, belly burn, broken pieces,
blood clots, parasites, foreign matter, excessive glaze, drip loss, or signs of decomposition.
Packaging integrity.
Label accuracy.
Evidence of thawing and refreezing.
Ice condition for chilled product.
Cleanliness of containers, totes, pallets, liners, bags, cartons, and reusable packaging.

# Page 14
Pest activity or contamination signs.
Chemical, fuel, smoke, cleaning agent, or oﬀ-odor exposure.
The following indicators shall trigger QA escalation:
Sour, ammonia-like, rancid, fuel-like, chemical, putrid, or abnormal odor.
Soft, mushy, dry, slimy, broken-down, or abnormal texture.
Unusual discoloration or decomposition signs.
Excessive temperature deviation.
Foreign matter or contamination.
Excessive damage or leakage.
Evidence of thawing in frozen product.
Inconsistent species identity.
Inconsistent label, certiﬁcation, or origin claim.
Signs of adulteration or substitution.
Supplier history requiring increased inspection.
Where organoleptic evaluation suggests possible food safety risk, product shall be placed on hold
pending QA decision. If required, samples shall be taken for laboratory testing.

14. Packaging, Labeling, and Quantity Veriﬁcation
Packaging and labeling shall be checked before acceptance. Minimum checks include:
Cartons, bags, totes, pallets, liners, and packaging are clean and intact.
Packaging protects product from contamination and temperature abuse.
Labels match product, species, lot, supplier, weight, presentation, storage condition, and
certiﬁcation claim.
Net weight or gross weight aligns with purchase and delivery documents.
Date codes, production codes, harvest dates, expiry dates, or best-before dates are present
where required.
Allergen or handling statements are present where required.
MSC or certiﬁcation labels are used only where supported by documentation.
Damaged or unlabelled units are segregated and reviewed.
Quantity and weight diﬀerences shall be recorded. Material discre

supported by documentation.
Damaged or unlabelled units are segregated and reviewed.
Quantity and weight diﬀerences shall be recorded. Material discrepancies shall be reported to
Procurement and Operations. If weight diﬀerences suggest substitution, loss, tampering, undeclared
thawing, water addition, glaze issue, or documentation inconsistency, QA shall be notiﬁed.

# Page 15
15. Sampling Requirements
Sampling shall be based on product type, risk level, supplier history, customer requirement, legal
requirement, and QA instructions. Sampling may include:
Visual inspection samples.
Temperature samples.
Sensory samples.
Weight veriﬁcation samples.
Microbiological samples.
Chemical or residue samples.
Histamine samples for susceptible species.
Species identiﬁcation samples where risk of substitution exists.
Retention samples where required by customer or internal policy.
Sampling tools shall be clean and suitable for food contact. Samples shall be identiﬁed with lot
information and handled to preserve integrity. Sampling shall not compromise product safety or
traceability. Any sample removed from a lot shall be recorded.

Rejection

16. Acceptance, Conditional Acceptance, Hold, and
Rejection
Receiving decisions shall be made according to documented evidence.
16.1 Acceptance
A lot may be accepted when:
Supplier and source are approved.
Documentation is complete and consistent.
Product identity and species are veriﬁed.
Temperature is within required limits or approved by QA after review.
Product condition meets speciﬁcation.
Packaging and labels are acceptable.
Traceability is complete.
Certiﬁcation claim is supported where applicable.
No unresolved food safety, quality, legal, security, or customer issue exists.

# Page 16
Accepted product shall be labeled, recorded, and moved to approved storage or processing area
without unnecessary delay.
16.2 Conditional Acceptance
Conditional acceptance may be allowed only with QA approval when the issue does not create
unacceptable food safety, legal, certiﬁcation, or customer risk. Examples include:
Minor document correction pending supplier conﬁrmation.
Minor packaging damage with product unaﬀected.
Minor temperature deviation with documented exposure assessment.
Quantity discrepancy under review.
Additional testing pending.
Supplier corrective action required but product can remain controlled.
Conditionally accepted product shall remain clearly identiﬁed and may require hold, restricted use,
sorting, rework, retesting, relabeling, downgraded use, or customer notiﬁcation before release.
16.3 Product Hold
Product shall be placed on hold when:
Acceptance cr

, retesting, relabeling, downgraded use, or customer notiﬁcation before release.
16.3 Product Hold
Product shall be placed on hold when:
Acceptance criteria are not fully met.
QA review is required.
Documentation is incomplete or inconsistent.
Temperature deviation needs assessment.
Product identity is uncertain.
Certiﬁcation claim is uncertain.
Security concern is unresolved.
Supplier status is unclear.
Testing is pending.
Rejection decision is being evaluated.
Held product shall be physically segregated where practical and identiﬁed in the system or manual
register. No held product may be processed, shipped, used, relabeled, reworked, or released without
written QA authorization.

# Page 17
16.4 Rejection
Product shall be rejected when mandatory requirements are not met and the risk cannot be
controlled. Rejection reasons may include:
Unapproved supplier or unauthorized vessel without approved exception.
Product unsafe or suspected unsafe.
Decomposition, contamination, adulteration, or severe quality failure.
Signiﬁcant temperature abuse.
Missing or false traceability.
Incorrect species or suspected substitution.
Invalid certiﬁcation claim.
Broken seal or security issue aﬀecting product integrity.
Regulatory document failure.
Customer-prohibited condition.
Product outside agreed speciﬁcation with no approved disposition.
Rejected product shall be labeled, segregated, recorded, and dispositioned according to QA,
Operations, Procurement, Legal/Compliance, and supplier agreement requirements.
17. Nonconformity and Corrective Action
All receiving nonconformities shall

tions, Procurement, Legal/Compliance, and supplier agreement requirements.
17. Nonconformity and Corrective Action
All receiving nonconformities shall be recorded. The record shall include:
Date and time.
Supplier and source.
Product and lot information.
Quantity aﬀected.
Description of issue.
Evidence, such as photos, temperature readings, labels, documents, or inspection notes.
Immediate action taken.
Product status.
Person reporting.
QA decision.
Supplier notiﬁcation where applicable.
Corrective action required.
Final disposition.

# Page 18
Recurring supplier issues shall be escalated to Procurement and QA for supplier performance review.
Corrective actions may include supplier warning, increased inspection, additional documentation,
corrective action request, suspension, removal from approved supplier list, revised speciﬁcation,
training, contract review, or change in sourcing.
18. Special Controls for Certiﬁed or Customer-
Speciﬁc Product
Product carrying MSC, customer sustainability, origin, social compliance, organic, export, or other
certiﬁcation claims shall receive additional veriﬁcation before acceptance.
Minimum controls include:
Supplier is eligible to supply certiﬁed or customer-speciﬁc product.
Certiﬁcation documents are current and match the product.
Lot identity supports claim.
Labels and records do not overstate claim.
Certiﬁed and non-certiﬁed product are segregated.
Traceability records support ma

identity supports claim.
Labels and records do not overstate claim.
Certiﬁed and non-certiﬁed product are segregated.
Traceability records support mass balance where applicable.
Any uncertainty results in hold until resolved.
If certiﬁed status cannot be veriﬁed, product shall not be received, processed, labeled, shipped, or
sold as certiﬁed. It may be accepted only as non-certiﬁed if QA, Traceability, Procurement, and
customer requirements allow that disposition.
19. Food Safety Hazard Considerations
Receiving inspection shall consider seafood-speciﬁc hazards, including:
Histamine formation in susceptible species.
Pathogenic microorganisms due to temperature abuse or contamination.
Parasites.
Chemical contamination from fuel, cleaning agents, lubricants, residues, or environmental
exposure.
Heavy metals or contaminants where applicable to species and source.
Allergens and cross-contact

nts, lubricants, residues, or environmental
exposure.

Allergens and cross-contact.
Foreign matter.
Decomposition.

# Page 19
Species substitution.
Illegal, unreported, or unregulated sourcing risk.
Poor sanitation during harvest, transport, landing, or handling.
The Food Safety Coordinator shall determine when product requires testing, enhanced inspection,
hold, rejection, or supplier investigation.
20. Hygiene, Safety, and Unloading Controls
Receiving personnel shall follow BSF-HSE-001 — Workplace Safety Procedure and applicable
hygiene rules during unloading and inspection.
Minimum controls include:
Required PPE is worn.
Hands are washed or sanitized according to site rules.
Personnel do not eat, drink, smoke, or store personal items in receiving areas.
Product is protected from ﬂoor contact, pests, splash, fumes, and chemicals.
Unloading equipment is clean and safe.
Damaged pallets, unstable loads, or unsafe stacking are corrected.
Cold-room doors are managed to reduce temperature loss.
Slips, tri

clean and safe.
Damaged pallets, unstable loads, or unsafe stacking are corrected.
Cold-room doors are managed to reduce temperature loss.
Slips, trips, cuts, crush risks, manual handling risks, and forklift movement are controlled.
Spills, leaks, ice, packaging waste, or broken product are cleaned promptly.
If unsafe conditions exist, receiving may be paused until the risk is controlled.

AUDIT READINESS
Audit readiness means evidence is current before the auditor asks.
Employees support audits by knowing where records are, why controls exist, and what to do when evidence is incomplete.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
10
Before audit
Confirm records are complete, versions are current, training is registered, and open actions have owners.
During audit
Answer only what you know, show evidence, explain actual practice, and escalate questions outside your role.
After audit
Review findings, define corrective actions, verify effectiveness, and update training or procedures.
Strong audit performance comes from normal discipline, not last-minute document cleanup.

# Slide 7
PURPOSE
Quality is controlled by process discipline, not inspection alone.
Every person who receives, handles, records, stores, ships, or supports seafood operations contributes to the QMS.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
03
Understand the system
Know why procedures, records, specifications, training, and approvals exist before work is performed.
Apply daily controls
Use approved methods for receiving, cold chain, hygiene, product status, traceability, and release decisions.
React correctly
Escalate nonconformities, protect product, preserve evidence, and support corrective action before risk spreads.
By the end, employees should understand how their role protects food safety, compliance, certification claims, and customer trust.

# Slide 8
QMS MAP
The QMS connects customer requirements to controlled evidence.
The system works when each process produces reliable records and clear decisions.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
04
01
Requirement
Customer, legal, certification, and internal expectations.
02
Procedure
Approved way of working for a defined process.
03
Execution
Trained personnel follow controls in real operations.
04
Record
Evidence confirms what happened and who approved it.
05
Improvement
Findings drive correction, prevention, and learning.
If any link is weak, BSF may lose traceability, release nonconforming product, or fail an audit.

# Slide 9
STANDARDS
External standards become practical controls inside BSF.
The induction explains what employees need to do, not the full text of each external standard.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
05
Reference
What employees should remember
ISO 9001
Follow approved processes, keep records, control changes, and improve from evidence.
HACCP
Prevent food safety hazards before product is accepted, processed, stored, or released.
BRCGS / IFS Food
Maintain hygiene, site standards, product control, traceability, and audit-ready records.
MSC Chain of Custody
Protect certified product identity, segregation, labeling, and claim integrity.
BASC
Control cargo, access, seals, suspicious activity, and supply-chain security evidence.
SMETA
Support safe, fair, responsible, and documented working conditions.

# Slide 10
RESPONSIBILITIES
Quality is owned by process owners and executed by everyone.
Accountability is shared, but each role has a distinct decision point.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
06
QA Manager
Defines quality controls, release rules, nonconformity treatment, audit evidence, and certification readiness.
Operations
Executes receiving, processing, storage, dispatch, hygiene, and cold-chain controls as approved.
Traceability
Maintains lot identity, certified status, records, labels, source data, and recall evidence.
All Personnel
Follow procedures, complete records, report deviations, protect product status, and ask before guessing.
A process is not controlled until the responsible person, required record, acceptance criteria, and escalation route are clear.

# Slide 11
DAILY CONTROLS
The most important QMS behaviors are visible on the floor.
Quality culture is built through repeated, documented, and supervised habits.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
07
Use only current procedures, forms, specifications, and approved master data.
Check product identity, status, temperature, hygiene, label, and storage condition before movement.
Record results immediately, accurately, legibly, and under the right lot or batch.
Keep accepted, rejected, held, certified, and non-certified product clearly separated.
Escalate missing documents, unusual product condition, broken seals, unclear labels, or temperature deviation.
Never release product when the acceptance criteria, approval authority, or traceability evidence is uncertain.
Simple rule
When quality status is unclear, the product is not released. It is protected, identified, and escalated.

# Slide 12
TRACEABILITY
Traceability depends on consistent identity from source to dispatch.
A traceable lot can be followed backward to origin and forward to customers using controlled evidence.

# Slide 13
NONCONFORMITIES
A nonconformity is a signal to protect the product and improve the system.
The first response is containment; the final response is verified corrective action.
BSF-QMS-001 | Quality Management Induction
09
1
Detect
Find deviation through inspection, audit, complaint, monitoring, or observation.
2
Contain
Stop release, identify affected product, protect evidence, and inform the owner.
3
Evaluate
Assess food safety, quality, legal, certification, customer, and security impact.
4
Correct
Fix immediate issue and define root-cause-based corrective action.
5
Verify
Confirm action effectiveness and update documents or training when needed.

Evidence Model Audit readiness depends on evidence that is complete, current, attributable, legible, retrievable, and aligned with the requirement it supports. Evidence must never be created after the fact to fill a gap without identifying the actual gap and corrective action. • Complete: includes all fields needed to understand the decision, result, date, person responsible, and follow-up. • Current: reflects the latest approved process, customer requirement, or certification scope. • Attributable: identifies the person, role, system, or source responsible for the record. • Legible and us

tomer requirement, or certification scope. • Attributable: identifies the person, role, system, or source responsible for the record. • Legible and usable: can be read, exported, indexed, or reviewed without ambiguity. • Retrievable: stored in an approved location with access aligned to document permissions. • Linked: connected to related evidence such as vessel authorization, receiving inspection, temperature monitoring, nonconformity, and release decision. 7. Audit Readiness Cycle BlueSea Foods maintains a practical audit readiness cycle. The goal is to avoid last-minute document collection and to make compliance part of normal operations. 1. Maintain the document inventory and confirm that each active document has an owner, format, review date, and access rule. 2. Review the compliance calendar monthly and identify certification audits, customer audits, supplier reviews, internal audi

w date, and access rule. 2. Review the compliance calendar monthly and identify certification audits, customer audits, supplier reviews, internal audits, and management review milestones. 3. Sample high-risk records every month: cold chain deviations, receiving holds, corrective actions, vessel authorizations, training records, and access changes. 4. Escalate gaps to the relevant owner with due date, risk level, evidence expected, and verification method.

ercises, workplace safety observations, and management review. 6. Classification and Severity Each nonconformity must be classified based on actual or potential impact. The classification determines containment urgency, approval level, due date, escalation, and verification requirements. Severity Criteria Required response Critical Potential food safety hazard, regulatory breach, fraudulent record, shipment of unsafe product, loss of traceability, undeclared allergen, or serious worker safety risk. Immediate containment, QA Manager and Senior Management notification, shipment stop if applicable, root cause within 3 business days. Major Systemic failure, repeated deviation, customer requirement breach, certification risk, supplier documentation failure, or product quality issue requiring formal CAPA. Containment within 24 hours, correcti

ment breach, certification risk, supplier documentation failure, or product quality issue requiring formal CAPA. Containment within 24 hours, corrective action plan within 5 business days, effectiveness verification required. Minor Isolated procedural gap, incomplete record with recoverable evidence, housekeeping issue, local training gap, or non-systemic administrative error. Correction within 5 business days and corrective action where recurrence risk exists. Observation Opportunity for improvement or early warning that does not yet represent a requirement failure. Preventive or improvement action assigned when useful; trend monitoring required. Food safety rule Any issue with possible food safety, legal, allergen, traceability, or intentional adulteration impact must be treated as at least Major until Quality Assurance completes the risk assessment. 7. ify HSE, investigate before restart. 7.3 Evaluate Product and Compliance Impact Quality Assurance must evaluate whether the nonconformity affects product disposition, customer notification, certification claims, regulatory obligations, supplier approval, recall risk, worker safety, or document integrity. • Identify affected product lots, dates, vessels, suppliers, production lines, customers, records, and inventory locations. • Review whether traceability is complete one step back and one step forward. • Check whether MSC Chain of Custody, BASC, HACCP, BRCGS, IFS Food, SMETA, or customer requirements are affected. • Determine whether regulatory or customer notification is required before product release or continued shipment. • Record the disposition decision: release, rework, downgrade, return, reject, destroy, re-label, re-inspect, or hold pending evidence. 7.4 Investigate Root Cause R

isposition decision: release, rework, downgrade, return, reject, destroy, re-label, re-inspect, or hold pending evidence. 7.4 Investigate Root Cause Root cause analysis must be proportional to risk. Minor isolated errors may use a simple cause statement, while major and critical cases require documented analysis such as 5 Whys, fishbone, process review, record review, staff interview, equipment check, or supplier investigation. The investigation must distinguish between the direct cause, the root cause, and the reason the issue escaped detection. Corrective actions that only retrain staff are not acceptable unless the investigation shows that the root cause was genuinely a training or competence gap.  BlueTrack User Manual 
 User guide for operating BlueTrack, the internal platform used by BlueSea Foods to manage receiving, inspection, cold chain control, vessel verification, traceability evidence, document access, alerts, and operational reporting.

1. Purpose 
 This manual explains how authorized BlueSea Foods personnel use BlueTrack to record, review, approve, monitor and retrieve operational information related to seafood receiving, inspection, cold chain control, vessel authorization, traceability, corrective actions, and document evidence. 
 BlueTrack is designed to support daily operational discipline. It does not replace professional judgment, legal requirements, food safety procedures, customer specifications or certified management system controls. Instead, it provides a controlled digital workspace where users can execute required steps, attach evidence, generate alerts, and maintain traceable records for internal management and external audits.

where users can execute required steps, attach evidence, generate alerts, and maintain traceable records for internal management and external audits.

Manual principle: If an operational event is not recorded in BlueTrack or in an approved contingency record, it may be treated as incomplete evidence during internal review, customer review or certification audit.

2. Scope 
 This manual applies to BlueSea Foods employees, supervisors, managers, approved contractors and temporary users who are granted access to BlueTrack. The system is used across the following process areas:

visors, managers, approved contractors and temporary users who are granted access to BlueTrack. The system is used across the following process areas:

Raw material receiving and inspection. 
 Cold chain temperature monitoring and deviation follow-up. 
 Supplier and authorized fishing vessel verification. 
 Lot creation, batch identification and traceability linkage. 
 Quality hold, release, rejection and corrective action workflows. 
 Operational dashboards and compliance reporting. 
 Controlled access to approved procedures, catalogs, registers and evidence files.

BlueTrack is not the system of record for payroll, personal employee benefits, commercial accounting, customer invoicing or legal contract execution unless a specific approved integration is documented by Information Technology and the relevant process owner.

3. Confidentiality 
 This document is classified as Internal Use Only . It contains operational instructions, system process descriptions and examples of internal workflows. Users shall not share this manual, screenshots, system exports, vessel data, supplier information, customer references, lot records or quality findings outside BlueSea Foods unless such sharing is authorized by the document owner and aligned with the applicable confidentiality requirements. 
 Users must avoid posting BlueTrack screenshots in personal messaging applications, social networks, external AI tools, unmanaged cloud folders or unapproved collaboration spaces. When support evidence must be shared with IT or management, users shall follow the approved support channel and remove unnecessary personal, supplier or customer data where possible.

5. User Roles and Permissions 
 Access in BlueTrack is role-based. Each user receives the minimum access required to perform assigned work. Role changes require approval from the process owner and validation by Information Technology.

Role Main Permissions Typical Users

Receiving Operator Create receiving entries, enter weights, attach receiving evidence, save drafts, submit records for Quality review. Receiving team, dock personnel, warehouse support. 
 Quality Inspector Record inspection results, place lots on hold, release or reject lots within authority, create nonconformity records. QC analysts, QA inspectors, food safety staff. 
 Cold Chain Monitor Enter and review temperature readings, record deviations, assign corrective actions, attach calibration evidence. Cold storage team, logistics, maintenance support. 
 Supply Chain Reviewer Review supplier and vessel status, validate permits, check MSC/BASC-related evidence, monitor expiration dates. Supply chain, sourcing, compliance coordinators. 
 Supervisor Approve selected workflows, reopen records where allowed, view team dashboards, assign follow-up actions. Operations supervisors, QA supervisors,

isor Approve selected workflows, reopen records where allowed, view team dashboards, assign follow-up actions. Operations supervisors, QA supervisors, warehouse leads. 
 Manager View dashboards, export reports, review KPIs, approve high-impact decisions when required by procedure. Operations, Quality, HSE, IT and senior management. 
 System Administrator Manage users, roles, master data, integrations, configuration and system availability monitoring. Authorized IT administrators only.

Important: Users must not share credentials, approve work using another user's account or request access outside their assigned responsibilities.

8. Receiving Module 
 The Receiving module is used to create the first controlled operational record for incoming seafood. The receiving record must be created as close as practical to the physical receipt of product and before any lot is moved to a controlled storage or production step. 
 8.1 Create a Receiving Record

s practical to the physical receipt of product and before any lot is moved to a controlled storage or production step. 
 8.1 Create a Receiving Record

Select Operations > Receiving > New Receiving Record . 
 Enter receiving date, facility, receiving bay, supplier, transport unit, product group and species. 
 Select the authorized vessel or supplier source where applicable. 
 Enter gross weight, tare weight, net weight, units, presentation and packaging type. 
 Record arrival temperature and attach the supporting temperature evidence. 
 Link required documents such as purchase order, transport document, certificate, landing declaration or supplier declaration. 
 Save as draft if data is incomplete or submit for Quality inspection if all mandatory fields are complete.

mplete or submit for Quality inspection if all mandatory fields are complete.

Supplier Must match an active supplier master record. Typing a similar commercial name instead of selecting the approved record. 
 Species Must match the controlled species catalog. Selecting a general product family when a specific species is required. 
 Vessel Required when the product is vessel-linked or MSC/BASC traceability applies. Leaving vessel blank because the supplier name was entered. 
 Temperature Must be entered with unit of measure and evidence. Uploading a photo but forgetting the numeric reading. 
 Lot ID Generated by BlueTrack after required fields are saved. Creating manual lot codes outside the system.

oto but forgetting the numeric reading.

9. Cold Chain Module 
 The Cold Chain module records product and storage temperature information. It supports monitoring of receiving temperature, storage conditions, transport transfer, corrective actions and trend review. 
 9.1 Enter Temperature Reading

orts monitoring of receiving temperature, storage conditions, transport transfer, corrective actions and trend review. 
 9.1 Enter Temperature Reading

Open the lot or storage location to be monitored. 
 Select Add Temperature Reading . 
 Enter date, time, reading value, unit, location, equipment ID and person responsible. 
 Attach supporting evidence when required by procedure or customer specification. 
 Select Save . BlueTrack will compare the value against the applicable limit.

9.2 Temperature Status

Status Meaning Required Action

specification. 
 Select Save . BlueTrack will compare the value against the applicable limit.

9.2 Temperature Status

Status Meaning Required Action

Within Limit The reading is within the approved range for the product/location. No deviation action required; continue monitoring. 
 Warning The reading is close to the limit or missing supporting data. Review promptly and add comments or evidence if needed. 
 Deviation The reading is outside the approved limit. Create or link corrective action, notify supervisor and Quality, and place product on hold if required.

Temperature deviations must be managed according to BSF-OPS-001 - Cold Chain Control Register and linked to quality disposition where product safety or customer requirements may be affected.

ccording to BSF-OPS-001 - Cold Chain Control Register and linked to quality disposition where product safety or customer requirements may be affected.

10. Quality Inspection Module 
 The Quality Inspection module is used to record sensory checks, physical condition, packaging condition, documentation conformity, temperature review and food safety observations. The inspection result determines whether a lot is released, rejected or placed on hold. 
 10.1 Complete an Inspection

ew and food safety observations. The inspection result determines whether a lot is released, rejected or placed on hold. 
 10.1 Complete an Inspection

Open the submitted receiving record from the Quality queue. 
 Confirm product identity, supplier, vessel, species, lot code and required documents. 
 Record inspection parameters: appearance, odor, texture, packaging, labeling, foreign material risk, temperature and sample observations. 
 Attach photographs or inspection forms where required. 
 Select a disposition: Release , Hold , Reject or Escalate . 
 If a nonconformity is detected, create a linked CAPA record before closing the inspection.

10.2 Disposition Rules

Disposition Use When System Impact

If a nonconformity is detected, create a linked CAPA record before closing the inspection.

10.2 Disposition Rules

Disposition Use When System Impact

Release Requirements are met and no blocking issue exists. Lot can proceed to the next authorized operation. 
 Hold Information, evidence, inspection result or risk review is incomplete. Lot is blocked until authorized review is completed. 
 Reject Product fails critical acceptance criteria or cannot be safely corrected. Lot is blocked and rejection workflow begins. 
 Escalate Decision requires manager, food safety team or customer input. Lot remains controlled pending higher-level decision.

ction workflow begins. 
 Escalate Decision requires manager, food safety team or customer input. Lot remains controlled pending higher-level decision.

11. Traceability and Vessel Verification 
 BlueTrack links product lots to approved suppliers, authorized vessels, species catalog records and relevant compliance evidence. This supports chain of custody, food safety traceability, supply chain security and audit readiness. 
 11.1 Verify an Authorized Vessel

ance evidence. This supports chain of custody, food safety traceability, supply chain security and audit readiness. 
 11.1 Verify an Authorized Vessel

Open Supply Chain > Authorized Vessels . 
 Search by vessel ID, vessel name, supplier, landing port or permit number. 
 Confirm status: Authorized, Conditional, Suspended, Expired or Under Review. 
 Review permit validity, owner/operator, applicable species and zone authorization. 
 Confirm whether MSC Chain of Custody, BASC or customer-specific controls apply. 
 Link the vessel record to the receiving lot or block the lot if the vessel is not valid for the intended operation.

16. Troubleshooting Guide

Issue Likely Cause User Action

Cannot log in Password issue, expired access, MFA problem or network restriction. Confirm credentials, retry MFA once, then contact IT support with screenshot and time of error. 
 Missing module Role profile does not include the module. Ask your manager to confirm whether access is required and submit an access change request. 
 Cannot submit record Mandatory field or attachment missing. Review highlighted fields, complete missing evidence and submit again. 
 Temperature status seems incorrect Wrong product, location or limit selected. Check species, storage location and temperature limit; escalate to Quality if still unclear. 
 Duplicate lot warning Similar supplier, date, species or transport record already exists. Do not bypass; review existing record and ask supervisor whether to merge, cancel or continue. 
 Export unavailable User does not have export permission or report is restric

xisting record and ask supervisor whether to merge, cancel or continue. 
 Export unavailable User does not have export permission or report is restricted. Request report through authorized supervisor or process owner.

Support ticket minimum data: include user name, module, record ID or lot ID, date/time, screenshot, browser/device, error message and business impact.

Emergency and Temporary Access Emergency access may be granted only when operational continuity, food safety, incident response, regulatory response, or critical customer support would be compromised by waiting for the standard workflow. Step Action 1 The requester documents the reason, system, user, permissions, expected duration, and approving manager. 2 IT grants the minimum permissions required and applies an expiration date wherever technically possible. 3 The system owner reviews activity logs after use and confirms that no unauthorized changes occurred. 4 Temporary access is removed immediately after the emergency or approved period ends. 5 Any exception or irregularity is recorded as an IT security event or nonconformity where appropriate.

by end date Line Manager and IT Service Desk 12. Access Incidents and Exceptions Any suspected unauthorized access, lost device, credential sharing, phishing attempt, unusual login, excessive permission, missing offboarding action, or uncontrolled data export must be reported immediately to IT and the relevant manager. Incident type Minimum response Credential compromise Reset credentials, revoke sessions, verify MFA, review logs, notify impacted owners. Unauthorized access Disable account or role, preserve evidence, investigate scope, raise CAPA if control failed. Lost or stolen device Remote lock or wipe if available, disable sessions, assess data exposure. Shared account use Stop use, assign named accounts, investigate record integrity, document exception if service account is approved. Delayed offboarding Disable access immediately, review activity since exit date, escalate to HR and

document exception if service account is approved. Delayed offboarding Disable access immediately, review activity since exit date, escalate to HR and management. Certification impact Access incidents affecting traceability, product release, document control, quality evidence, or certification claims must be evaluated for possible nonconformity under BSF-QMS-003. 13. ion. Emergency Access Log Reason, approver, permissions, duration, activity review, removal confirmation. Security Incident Record Issue, containment, investigation, impact, actions, evidence, closure. KPIs and Monitoring KPI Target Frequency Owner New access implemented with approval 100% Monthly IT Manager Offboarding completed on time 100% for immediate exits; 98% or higher for standard exits Monthly HR and IT Privileged access reviewed 100% by scheduled review date Quarterly IT Manager Temporary access overdue 0 overdue without documented exception Monthly IT Service Desk Access incidents closed Closed by severity target with evidence Monthly IT Manager MFA coverage 100% for required systems Monthly IT S

IT Service Desk Access incidents closed Closed by severity target with evidence Monthly IT Manager MFA coverage 100% for required systems Monthly IT Security Owner 15. Risk Controls Risk Impact Control Excessive permissions Users may alter records, release product, or view confidential data outside their role. Least privilege, system owner approval, periodic review. Delayed offboarding Former personnel retain access to systems or confidential records. HR-triggered exit workflow and immediate disablement for urgent exits. Shared credentials Loss of accountability and unreliable audit trail. Unique user identity, service account approval, disciplinary escalation where needed. Weak MFA adoption Higher risk of unauthorized external access. MFA mandatory for high-risk systems and periodic compliance reporting. Uncontrolled exports Leakage of supplier, customer, payroll, certification, or prod

mandatory for high-risk systems and periodic compliance reporting. Uncontrolled exports Leakage of supplier, customer, payroll, certification, or product traceability data. Export restrictions, approved repositories, user training, audit logs. Incomplete evidence Audit failure because access approval or review cannot be demonstrated. Ticket evidence standards and document controller retention checks. 16.
